---
title: "Chapter 7: Publication-Quality Charts"
subtitle: "R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Apply **professional themes** for clean, branded charts
- Customize **labels, colors, and scales** for stakeholder presentations
- Use **facet_wrap()** and **facet_grid()** for small multiples
- Combine charts into **multi-panel layouts** with `patchwork`
- Export charts at **publication resolution** with `ggsave()`
- Define a **BK Advisors color palette** for consistent branding
:::


## Chapter Overview

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">Themes</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #00A1DF;">Labels &<br>Scales</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">Colors</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;">Faceting</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">Patchwork</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #E24A3F;">Exporting</div>
</div>


# Themes --- Professional Look {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">From default gray to presentation-ready</span>


## Built-in Themes

```{r}
#| label: themes
#| echo: true

library(ggplot2)

p <- ggplot(country_summary, aes(x = reorder(country, avg_nmr), y = avg_nmr)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#005CB9") +
  labs(title = "NMR by Country", x = NULL, y = "NMR")

# Default theme (gray background, grid lines)
p + theme_gray()

# Clean white theme (recommended for presentations)
p + theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

# Classic theme (axes only, no grid)
p + theme_classic(base_size = 14)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
`theme_minimal(base_size = 14)` is the recommended starting point. The `base_size` argument scales all text proportionally --- no more adjusting each element one at a time.
:::


## Customizing Theme Elements

```{r}
#| label: custom-theme
#| echo: true

p + theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    # Title styling
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#005CB9"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#666666"),

    # Axis styling
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0),

    # Grid: keep horizontal, remove vertical
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),

    # Legend
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
Every element on a ggplot can be customized with `theme()`. Use `element_text()` for text, `element_line()` for lines, `element_rect()` for backgrounds, and `element_blank()` to remove an element entirely.
:::


## A Reusable BKA Theme

```{r}
#| label: bka-theme
#| echo: true

# Define once, use everywhere
theme_bka <- function(base_size = 14) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#005CB9", size = rel(1.2)),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#666666", size = rel(0.9)),
      plot.caption = element_text(color = "#999999", size = rel(0.7)),
      axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#005CB9")
    )
}

# Use it
ggplot(country_summary, aes(x = reorder(country, avg_nmr), y = avg_nmr)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#005CB9") +
  labs(title = "Neonatal Mortality Rate by Country",
       subtitle = "Average across all reporting facilities",
       caption = "Source: Facility-level data, 2024") +
  theme_bka()
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
Wrapping your theme in a function means every chart in your project starts from the same baseline. When stakeholders request a style change, you update one function --- not dozens of charts.
:::


# Labels & Scales {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Making charts self-explanatory</span>


## labs() --- Titles, Subtitles, and Captions

```{r}
#| label: labs
#| echo: true

ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Facility Delivery Volume vs. Neonatal Mortality",
    subtitle = "Each point is one health facility (n = 200)",
    x = "Monthly Delivery Volume",
    y = "Neonatal Mortality Rate (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = "Country",
    caption = "Source: MNH Program Data, 2024 | BK Advisors"
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
Every chart for a stakeholder presentation should have a **title** (what the chart shows), a **subtitle** (context or sample size), and a **caption** (data source). The `labs()` function handles all three in one call.
:::


## scale_* Functions --- Controlling Axes

```{r}
#| label: scales
#| echo: true

library(scales)

# Format y-axis as percentage
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = country, y = anc_coverage)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#00A1DF", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "ANC Coverage Distribution by Country",
       y = "ANC Coverage (%)")

# Format y-axis with commas for large numbers
ggplot(country_summary, aes(x = country, y = total_deliveries)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#83BD00") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Total Deliveries by Country", y = "Deliveries")
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
The `scales` package provides `percent`, `comma`, `dollar`, and `date_format()` --- ready-made formatters that turn raw numbers into labels your stakeholders expect. No manual string manipulation needed.
:::


# Color Palettes {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Consistent branding with BK Advisors colors</span>


## BK Advisors Color Palette

```{r}
#| label: bka-colors
#| echo: true

# BKA brand colors
bka_blue       <- "#005CB9"
bka_light_blue <- "#00A1DF"
bka_green      <- "#83BD00"
bka_teal       <- "#3E9B6E"
bka_red        <- "#E24A3F"
bka_orange     <- "#FA7650"
bka_amber      <- "#F8A623"
bka_yellow     <- "#FED141"

# Named palette for countries
bka_country_colors <- c(
  "Ethiopia"   = bka_blue,
  "Tanzania"   = bka_teal,
  "Malawi"     = bka_green,
  "Mozambique" = bka_orange,
  "Kenya"      = bka_light_blue
)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
Defining your palette as a named vector means you can reuse it across every chart in a project. Each country always gets the same color --- no more remembering which hex code goes where.
:::


## Applying the Palette

```{r}
#| label: apply-palette
#| echo: true

# Manual color scale for specific categories
ggplot(country_trends, aes(x = month, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = bka_country_colors) +
  labs(title = "NMR Trends by Country",
       x = "Month", y = "NMR", color = NULL) +
  theme_bka()

# Manual fill scale for bar charts
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = facility_type, fill = facility_type)) +
  geom_bar() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Hospital" = bka_blue,
                                "Health Center" = bka_teal,
                                "Health Post" = bka_green)) +
  labs(title = "Facilities by Type") +
  theme_bka() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
Use `scale_color_manual()` for points and lines. Use `scale_fill_manual()` for bars, histograms, and areas. They work the same way --- the only difference is which aesthetic they target.
:::


# Faceting --- Small Multiples {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">One chart per group</span>


## facet_wrap() --- One Variable

```{r}
#| label: facet-wrap
#| echo: true

# NMR histogram, one panel per country
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = nmr)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "#005CB9", color = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "NMR Distribution by Country",
       x = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
       y = "Facilities") +
  theme_bka()
```

. . .

::: {.example-block}
<div class="example-label">When to use facet_wrap()</div>

Split by **one categorical variable** --- country, facility type, year, or region. Use `ncol` or `nrow` to control the layout. Each panel shares the same axes, making comparison straightforward.
:::


## facet_grid() --- Two Variables

```{r}
#| label: facet-grid
#| echo: true

# NMR by delivery volume, split by country (rows) and facility type (columns)
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr)) +
  geom_point(color = "#005CB9", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "#E24A3F") +
  facet_grid(country ~ facility_type) +
  labs(title = "Delivery Volume vs. NMR",
       subtitle = "By country and facility type") +
  theme_bka()
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
Faceting is one of the most powerful visualization techniques. Instead of cramming everything into one chart, show the same chart for each group. The pattern (or lack thereof) becomes obvious.
:::


## Free vs. Fixed Scales

```{r}
#| label: facet-scales
#| echo: true

# Fixed scales (default) — same axis range for all panels
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = nmr)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "#005CB9", color = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Fixed Scales: Easy to Compare Magnitudes") +
  theme_bka()

# Free scales — each panel gets its own axis range
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = nmr)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "#005CB9", color = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 3, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title = "Free Y-Axis: Better for Seeing Each Distribution") +
  theme_bka()
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
Use `scales = "free_y"` when group sizes differ dramatically. Use the default (fixed) when you want stakeholders to compare absolute magnitudes across groups.
:::


# Multi-Panel Layouts with patchwork {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Combining charts into dashboard-style layouts</span>


## patchwork Basics

```{r}
#| label: patchwork
#| echo: true

library(patchwork)

# Create individual charts
p1 <- ggplot(country_summary, aes(x = reorder(country, avg_nmr), y = avg_nmr)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#005CB9") +
  labs(title = "NMR by Country", x = NULL, y = "NMR") +
  theme_bka()

p2 <- ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = nmr)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "#3E9B6E", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "NMR Distribution", x = "NMR", y = "Count") +
  theme_bka()

p3 <- ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = delivery_volume, y = nmr)) +
  geom_point(color = "#00A1DF", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Volume vs. NMR", x = "Deliveries", y = "NMR") +
  theme_bka()

# Combine: side by side
p1 + p2

# Stack vertically
p1 / p2

# Complex layout
(p1 + p2) / p3 +
  plot_annotation(title = "MNH Program Dashboard",
                  subtitle = "Facility-Level Analysis")
```


## patchwork Layout Operators

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}

| Operator | Layout |
|----------|--------|
| `p1 + p2` | Side by side |
| `p1 / p2` | Stacked vertically |
| `(p1 + p2) / p3` | Two on top, one below |
| `p1 + p2 + p3 + plot_layout(ncol = 1)` | All stacked |

:::

::: {.col}

```{r}
#| label: patchwork-annotate
#| echo: true

# Add shared title and tag each panel
(p1 + p2) / p3 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "MNH Program Dashboard",
    subtitle = "Facility-Level Analysis",
    caption = "Source: MNH Data, 2024",
    tag_levels = "A"   # Labels: A, B, C
  )
```

:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
`patchwork` replaces the manual labor of arranging charts in PowerPoint. Each panel is a reproducible ggplot object --- update the data and the entire dashboard rebuilds automatically.
:::


# Exporting Charts {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">From R to PowerPoint and reports</span>


## ggsave() --- Save to File

```{r}
#| label: ggsave
#| echo: true

# Save the last plot
ggsave("nmr_by_country.png",
       width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 300)

# Save a specific plot object
ggsave("dashboard.png", plot = (p1 + p2) / p3,
       width = 14, height = 10, dpi = 300)

# PDF for print
ggsave("nmr_trend.pdf", width = 10, height = 6)

# SVG for web
ggsave("nmr_trend.svg", width = 10, height = 6)
```


## Output Format Guide

| Format | Use Case | Argument |
|--------|----------|----------|
| PNG | Presentations, email, web | `"chart.png"` |
| PDF | Print reports, papers | `"chart.pdf"` |
| SVG | Web, scalable graphics | `"chart.svg"` |

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
Always use `dpi = 300` for presentation and print. The default (72 dpi) looks pixelated on slides. For PowerPoint, save as PNG at 300 dpi and insert the image --- it will look crisp on any projector.
:::


## Sizing Charts for Different Outputs

```{r}
#| label: ggsave-sizes
#| echo: true

# Full-width slide (16:9 aspect ratio)
ggsave("slide_chart.png", width = 12, height = 6.75, dpi = 300)

# Half-width (for side-by-side in a report)
ggsave("half_chart.png", width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 300)

# A4 page (portrait, with margins)
ggsave("report_chart.pdf", width = 7, height = 9)

# Square (for social media or dashboards)
ggsave("square_chart.png", width = 8, height = 8, dpi = 300)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
The `width` and `height` arguments are in **inches** by default. Set them to match your final output --- a 16:9 slide is roughly 12 x 6.75 inches. Getting this right avoids stretched or squished text.
:::


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**A reusable theme function (`theme_bka()`) and a named color palette ensure every chart is on-brand.**

**Faceting with `facet_wrap()` is the fastest way to compare the same pattern across countries or facility types.**

**`patchwork` combines individual charts into dashboard layouts --- replace your PowerPoint chart grids with reproducible code.**
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 8 --- Descriptive Statistics for Consultants**

- Means, medians, and proportions
- Confidence intervals
- Correlation and simple regression
- Interpreting regression output
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[R for Management Consultants | Chapter 7: Publication-Quality Charts]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
